claims.claim_text: The OTCA exam weights The OpenTelemetry API and SDK at 46% of the exam.
claims.span_hint: line 9
claims.rationale: The official CNCF OTCA exam page lists The OpenTelemetry API and SDK domain at 46%.

claims.claim_text: The Kubernetes 1.35+-specific curriculum/deployment generalization is more specific than the allowlisted primary sources support.
claims.span_hint: line 54
claims.anchor_text: Although the OTCA exam is not a Kubernetes operations test, most production OpenTelemetry deployments you meet in this curriculum will run beside Kubernetes 1.35+ workloads, collectors, sidecars, gateways, and admission-controlled namespaces.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Although the OTCA exam is not a Kubernetes operations test, many OpenTelemetry deployments in practice run alongside Kubernetes workloads, collectors, sidecars, and gateways.
claims.rationale: The operational lesson is fine, but the exact Kubernetes version and curriculum-wide frequency claim are not directly backed by an allowlisted primary source.

claims.claim_text: OpenTelemetry separates the API imported by instrumentation code from the SDK implementation managed by the application owner.
claims.span_hint: lines 40-42 | section: Why This Module Matters
claims.rationale: The OpenTelemetry overview specification explicitly distinguishes API packages from the SDK implementation.

claims.claim_text: Traces are built around TracerProviders, Tracers, exporters, span context, span kinds, status, events, and links for causal relationships that do not fit a simple parent-child tree.
claims.span_hint: lines 65-68, 103-129, 211-245, 280-283 | sections: Part 1 and Part 2
claims.rationale: The traces concepts page directly covers TracerProvider, exporters, span context, span kinds, status, events, and links.

claims.claim_text: Metrics are organized around MeterProviders, Meters, exporters, and instrument kinds such as counters, up-down counters, histograms, and asynchronous instruments with distinct semantics.
claims.span_hint: lines 142-167, 300-325 | section: Part 1 and Part 3
claims.rationale: The metrics concepts page defines MeterProvider, Meter, exporters, and the standard instrument kinds and semantics.

claims.claim_text: Metric temporality distinguishes cumulative from delta reporting, and exemplars can attach trace context such as trace_id and span_id to metric events.
claims.span_hint: lines 327-366 | section: Part 3: Metrics That Preserve Meaning
claims.rationale: The metrics data model specification defines delta vs cumulative temporality and exemplars with trace_id and span_id.

claims.claim_text: OpenTelemetry log integration preserves existing logging approaches and correlates logs with traces by carrying TraceId and SpanId in log records.
claims.span_hint: lines 169-172 | section: Part 1: The SDK Pipeline Mental Model
claims.rationale: The logs concepts page explains log bridges/appender-style integration and log-to-trace correlation.

claims.claim_text: Simple span processing exports spans one by one, while batching exports spans in groups and is the recommended default shape for production use.
claims.span_hint: lines 125-129, 204-206 | section: Part 1: The SDK Pipeline Mental Model
claims.rationale: The official exporters documentation contrasts simple and batched processing and explicitly recommends batching.

claims.claim_text: The batch span processor is configured with environment variables including OTEL_BSP_MAX_QUEUE_SIZE, OTEL_BSP_SCHEDULE_DELAY, and OTEL_BSP_MAX_EXPORT_BATCH_SIZE.
claims.span_hint: lines 136-140 | section: Part 1: The SDK Pipeline Mental Model
claims.rationale: The SDK environment variable specification defines these exact batch span processor variables.

claims.claim_text: A periodic exporting MetricReader collects metrics on a configured interval and passes them to a push exporter.
claims.span_hint: lines 163-167 | section: Part 1: The SDK Pipeline Mental Model
claims.rationale: The metrics SDK specification defines the periodic exporting MetricReader as the push-oriented reader implementation.

claims.claim_text: A Prometheus exporter is pull-based and responds to scrape requests over HTTP rather than initiating exports itself.
claims.span_hint: lines 156-159, 165-166 | section: Part 1: The SDK Pipeline Mental Model
claims.rationale: The Prometheus exporter specification explicitly defines it as a pull metric exporter that responds to HTTP requests.

claims.claim_text: Exceptions are recorded as span events and failed operations should set span status to ERROR rather than relying on exception recording alone.
claims.span_hint: lines 270-271 | section: Part 2: Span Anatomy and Trace Design
claims.rationale: The exceptions specification shows exceptions recorded as events and the paired setting of span status to ERROR.

claims.claim_text: Resources identify the emitting entity, are attached at provider initialization, and service.name is a resource attribute with an unknown_service fallback when unset.
claims.span_hint: lines 255-258 and 475-476 | sections: Part 2 and Part 5
claims.rationale: The resources concepts page directly states that resources are attached at provider creation and documents service.name plus the unknown_service fallback.

claims.claim_text: OpenTelemetry's default propagator uses W3C TraceContext, and traceparent carries version, trace ID, parent ID, and trace flags across service boundaries.
claims.span_hint: lines 402-425 | section: Part 4: Context Propagation and Baggage
claims.rationale: The context propagation page states that the default propagator uses W3C TraceContext and shows the traceparent field layout.

claims.claim_text: Baggage propagates application key-value data downstream and can expose sensitive values because it is commonly transmitted in network headers.
claims.span_hint: lines 427-430 and 434-438 | section: Part 4: Context Propagation and Baggage
claims.rationale: The baggage documentation explicitly warns that sensitive baggage can be shared unintentionally because baggage is sent in request headers.

claims.claim_text: General SDK environment variables include OTEL_SERVICE_NAME, OTEL_RESOURCE_ATTRIBUTES, OTEL_TRACES_SAMPLER, OTEL_TRACES_SAMPLER_ARG, and OTEL_TRACES_EXPORTER, with OTEL_SERVICE_NAME taking precedence over the same key in OTEL_RESOURCE_ATTRIBUTES.
claims.span_hint: lines 455-481 | section: Part 5: SDK Configuration and Export Choices
claims.rationale: The general SDK configuration page documents these exact variables and the OTEL_SERVICE_NAME precedence rule.

claims.claim_text: Signal-specific OTLP endpoint and protocol settings override general OTLP settings, with typical SDK defaults of port 4317 for gRPC and 4318 for HTTP.
claims.span_hint: lines 468-481 | section: Part 5: SDK Configuration and Export Choices
claims.rationale: The OTLP exporter configuration page documents general and signal-specific endpoint/protocol variables and the usual 4317/4318 defaults.

claims.claim_text: The OpenTelemetry Collector is vendor-agnostic and is designed to receive, process, and export telemetry to one or more backends.
claims.span_hint: lines 483-486 | section: Part 5: SDK Configuration and Export Choices
claims.rationale: The Collector introduction explicitly describes it as a vendor-agnostic component that can process and export to one or more backends.

claims.claim_text: Current stable HTTP semantic conventions use attributes such as http.request.method and http.response.status_code, and older names can still appear in older examples or dashboards.
claims.span_hint: lines 496-499 | section: Part 5: SDK Configuration and Export Choices
claims.rationale: The HTTP semantic-convention migration guide directly maps older HTTP attribute names to the current stable forms.

claims.claim_text: Java zero-code instrumentation uses a Java agent JAR attached to the JVM and dynamically injects bytecode.
claims.span_hint: line 525 | section: Part 6: Auto-Instrumentation, Manual Instrumentation, and the Boundary Between Them
claims.rationale: The Java agent page explicitly describes zero-code Java instrumentation as a Java agent that dynamically injects bytecode.

claims.claim_text: Python zero-code instrumentation commonly runs through the opentelemetry-instrument wrapper command.
claims.span_hint: line 526 | section: Part 6: Auto-Instrumentation, Manual Instrumentation, and the Boundary Between Them
claims.rationale: The Python zero-code documentation shows configuration and launch through the opentelemetry-instrument command.

claims.claim_text: .NET automatic instrumentation uses CLR profiler support and/or DOTNET_STARTUP_HOOKS-based startup hooks.
claims.span_hint: line 527 | section: Part 6: Auto-Instrumentation, Manual Instrumentation, and the Boundary Between Them
claims.rationale: The .NET automatic-instrumentation configuration docs explicitly document CLR profiler variables and DOTNET_STARTUP_HOOKS.

claims.claim_text: Node.js zero-code instrumentation can be enabled with NODE_OPTIONS=--require @opentelemetry/auto-instrumentations-node/register.
claims.span_hint: line 528 | section: Part 6: Auto-Instrumentation, Manual Instrumentation, and the Boundary Between Them
claims.rationale: The JavaScript zero-code documentation gives this exact NODE_OPTIONS-based require-hook pattern.

further_reading.title: OpenTelemetry Traces Concepts
further_reading.why_relevant: Best single reference for span context, span kinds, links, status, and the trace pipeline mental model used throughout the module.

further_reading.title: OpenTelemetry Metrics Data Model
further_reading.why_relevant: This is the strongest source for temporality, exemplars, and how aggregated metric points preserve meaning.

further_reading.title: OpenTelemetry Context Propagation
further_reading.why_relevant: It directly explains traceparent, parent-child continuity across services, and the default TraceContext-based propagator model.

further_reading.title: OTLP Exporter Configuration
further_reading.why_relevant: This is the cleanest official reference for endpoint precedence, protocol choices, and per-signal OTLP configuration.

notes: No shared citation pool appears to exist for this OTCA module family, so section_pool_ref is null and source_ids are empty. I skipped analogies, quiz prompts, hands-on exercise steps, code blocks, and purely pedagogical connective prose unless nearby prose made a load-bearing factual standards/tool-behavior claim.
